1: vayedaber YHWH el-Moshe ve'el-Aharon lemor: YHWH addresses both Moses and Aaron for the red heifer law — as in Numbers 18 (priestly duties), this law involves both prophetic and priestly leadership together. The joint address signals that what follows bridges civil and priestly jurisdiction.
2: zot chukat hatorah: 'this is the statute of the Torah.' Chukat (statute/decree) designates a law whose rationale is not provided — it is obeyed because YHWH commands it, not because its logic is evident. The rabbis called the red heifer the archetypal chok (unexplained statute): it purifies the impure while rendering the pure impure. parah adumah: 'a red cow/heifer' — parah (cow) + adumah (red; from the root adam, same root as Adam, adamah earth, dam blood). The red color connects to blood and earth. asher lo-alah alehah ol: 'upon which a yoke has never come' — unblemished AND unworked; both requirements echo the sacrificial perfection standard.
3: netatum otah el-Eleazar hakohen vehotzi otah el-michutz lamachaneh ushachat otah lefanav: 'give it to Eleazar the priest, and he shall take it outside the camp and slaughter it before him.' Two notable features: (1) The slaughter is done by Eleazar, not Aaron — the high priest is not directly involved, perhaps because the law involves corpse-impurity (which the high priest must strictly avoid, Lev 21:10-12). (2) El-michutz lamachaneh: outside the camp — a key spatial marker (cf. Lev 4:12, 21; Heb 13:11-13). Purity transactions happen outside the holy zone.
4: velakach Eleazar hakohen midamah be'etzba'o vehizah el-nochach penei ohel-mo'ed midamah sheva pe'amim: 'Eleazar the priest shall take some of its blood with his finger and sprinkle some of its blood toward the front of the tent of meeting seven times.' The sevenfold blood-sprinkling toward the tabernacle — identical to the sin offering's blood-rite (Lev 4:6,17). The red heifer blood is not brought inside the tent but sprinkled toward it: a sin-offering motion performed for the whole community's ongoing purification.
5: vesaraf et-haparah le'einav et-oroh ve'et-besarah ve'et-damah al-pirhah yisrof: 'the heifer shall be burned in his sight — its hide, its flesh, its blood, and its dung.' The entire animal is burned — no portion for the priest, no peace-offering meal, no altar burning; the whole heifer becomes ash. Pirhah (its dung) — even the contents of the intestines are burned; the completeness is absolute. The wholeness of the burning mirrors the olah (burnt offering) principle: total consumption, total transformation into ash.
6: velakach hakohen etz erez ve'ezov usheni tola'at veheshtlich el-toch srefat haparah: 'the priest shall take cedarwood and hyssop and scarlet yarn, and throw them into the fire of the burning heifer.' The three materials — cedar, hyssop, scarlet — are the identical materials used in the purification of skin disease (Lev 14:4,6,49-51). The overlap signals that the red heifer purification addresses the same category of deep-seated impurity: death-like contamination requiring radical cleansing.
7: vechibes begadav hakohen verachatz besaro bamayim ve'achar yavo el-hamachaneh vetame hakohen ad-ha'arev: 'the priest shall wash his clothes and bathe his body in water and afterward come into the camp. The priest shall be unclean until the evening.' The paradox appears: the priest who performs the purification rite becomes impure. He who cleanses others becomes unclean. Tamei ad-ha'arev (unclean until evening) — temporary but real. Hebrews 9:13-14 uses this paradox: 'If the blood of goats and bulls... sanctify for the purification of the flesh, how much more will the blood of Christ... purify our conscience from dead works?'
8: vehasaref otah yechabes begadav bemayim verachatz besaro bamayim vetame ad-ha'arev: 'the one who burns it shall wash his clothes in water and bathe his body in water and be unclean until the evening.' The person who burns the heifer also becomes unclean — though the burning was the divinely commanded ritual act. Both the priest (blood-sprinkling) and the lay assistant (burning) are rendered impure by the purifying act. The paradox deepens: the instrument of purification is itself defiled by the contact with death-pollution it processes.
9: ve'asaf ish tahor et efer haparah vehiniach michutz lamachaneh bemakom tahor vehayetah le'adat bnei-Yisrael lemishmeret lemei niddah chata'at hi: 'a man who is clean shall gather up the ashes of the heifer and deposit them outside the camp in a clean place. And they shall be kept for the water for impurity [mei niddah] for the congregation of Israel; it is a sin offering [chata'at].' The ashes are designated as both mei niddah (purification/impurity water — niddah means separation/impurity) and chata'at (sin offering). The heifer's ash is a standing sin offering held in reserve for community use — a permanent atoning resource deposited outside the camp.
10: vechibes ha'osef et-efer haparah et-begadav vetame ad-ha'arev: 'the one who gathers the ashes of the heifer shall wash his clothes and be unclean until the evening.' The paradox is complete: even the gatherer of the purifying ashes becomes impure by the gathering. Four categories of people — the priest (v7), the burner (v8), the ash-gatherer (v10), and the sprinkler (v21) — all incur temporary uncleanness through handling the purifying material. The mechanism that purifies cannot be handled without cost.
11: hanogea bemet lenefesh kol-adam yitma shivat yamim: 'whoever touches the dead body of any person shall be unclean seven days.' Corpse-impurity (tum'at met) — the most severe category of ritual impurity in the Levitical system — lasts seven days, requiring two applications of purification water (days 3 and 7). Nefesh adam (human soul/person) — the noun nefesh used for the corpse: the life (nefesh) has departed, leaving defilement.
12: hu yitchata-bo bayom hashlishi uvayom hashvi'i yithar: 'he shall cleanse himself with it on the third day and on the seventh day, and so be clean.' The two-application structure — days 3 and 7 — may encode the death-to-resurrection pattern: day 3 (an intermediate purification, anticipating the day of resurrection; cf. the third-day pattern in OT typology) and day 7 (complete Sabbath-rest purity). If he does not cleanse himself on these specific days, the impurity persists.
13: kol-hanogea bemet benefesh ha'adam asher yamut velo yitchata et-mishkan YHWH timeh: 'whoever touches a dead person, the body of anyone who has died, and does not cleanse himself, defiles the tabernacle of YHWH.' The severity: corpse-impurity that goes uncleansed defiles the central sanctuary. The impure individual's presence in the camp threatens the holy space at the center. Ki mei niddah lo-zorak alav: because the purification water was not sprinkled on him. The uncleansed is cut off (nikretah ha-nefesh hahi miyisrael).
14: zot hatorah adam ki yamut be'ohel: 'this is the law: when a person dies in a tent.' The tent-death rule: everything in the tent and everyone who comes into the tent becomes unclean seven days. Ohel (tent) — the portable dwelling; in the wilderness context, all dwellings are tents. The tent that contains death spreads death-impurity to all it contains. This rule will later apply to houses in the land (Lev 14:33-45).
15: vekhol keli patu'ach asher ein-tzamid pasil alav tameh hu: 'every open vessel with no cover fastened on it is unclean.' Open containers in a tent with a corpse absorb the impurity. Covered vessels are protected. The detail reflects the practical concern of preventing food and utensils from becoming defiled by proximity to death.
16: vekhol asher yiga al-penei hasadeh bechalalei-cherev o bemet o be'etzem adam o bekever yitma shivat yamim: 'whoever in the open field touches one who has been killed with a sword or who has died naturally, or touches a human bone or a grave, shall be unclean seven days.' The corpse-impurity law extends to the open field: battlefield dead, natural deaths, bones, and graves all convey impurity. Chalal cherev (slain by sword) — battlefield deaths; et etzem adam o kever (human bone or grave). Even touching a grave conveys impurity — this is the background to Jesus's 'whitewashed tombs' accusation (Matt 23:27): Pharisees, who seek to appear righteous externally while internally spreading death, are like the graves that invisibly communicate impurity to those who unknowingly walk over them.
17: velakhu latameh meavar hasreifah hachatat venatan alav mayim chayyim el-keli: 'for the unclean they shall take some of the ashes of the burned sin offering and add fresh water to them in a vessel.' Avar hasreifah (from the dust of the burning): the ash of the heifer combined with mayim chayyim (living/fresh water — flowing spring water, not stagnant) creates the purification solution. The paradox continues: inert ash + living water = purifying liquid.
18: velakach ezov vetaval bamayim ish tahor vehizah al-ha'ohel ve'al kol-hakeilim ve'al hanefashot asher hayu sham: 'a clean person shall take hyssop and dip it in the water and sprinkle it on the tent and on all the furnishings and on the persons who were there.' Ezov (hyssop) — the sprinkling implement (cf. Exod 12:22 Passover; Ps 51:7: 'purify me with hyssop, and I shall be clean'). Hyssop appears at Passover, in skin disease purification, and here — consistently associated with blood-and-water cleansing. The hyssop reappears at the crucifixion (John 19:29).
19: vehizah hatahor al-hatameh bayom hashlishi uvayom hashvi'i vechite'o vayom hashvi'i vecibes begadav verachatz bamayim vetaher ba'arev: 'the clean person shall sprinkle it on the unclean on the third day and on the seventh day. Thus on the seventh day he shall cleanse him, and he shall wash his clothes and bathe himself in water, and at evening he shall be clean.' The purification process: sprinkled day 3 and day 7; washed on day 7; clean at evening. The progression from impure to clean mirrors the creation-week structure, ending at the Sabbath moment (day 7 evening).
20: ve'ish asher yitma velo yitchata venikretah ha-nefesh hahi mitkoch hakahal: 'but the man who is unclean and does not cleanse himself, that person shall be cut off from the midst of the assembly.' Venikretah (cut off/karet) — the covenant sanction for refusing purification. The one who refuses the provision for cleansing is cut off; the provision itself is not the problem, but refusing it is fatal. The red heifer's ash is the provision YHWH has made; ignoring it costs the covenant relationship.
21: vetahayetah lahem lechukat olam umaze mei haniddah yechabes begadav vehanogea bemei haniddah yitma ad-ha'arev: 'and it shall be a statute forever for them. The one who sprinkles the water for impurity shall wash his clothes, and the one who touches the water for impurity shall be unclean until the evening.' Even the sprinkler incurs temporary impurity. The eternal statute (chukat olam) governs this: the mechanism that cleanses cannot itself be touched without cost. The paradox is permanent — built into creation-order.
22: vekhol asher yiga bo hatameh yitma vehanefesh hanogaat tetama ad-ha'arev: 'whatever the unclean person touches shall be unclean, and whoever touches it shall be unclean until the evening.' The impurity transfer principle: the unclean communicates impurity by touch; the contamination extends through a single contact chain. This is why the red heifer law is necessary: without systematic purification, impurity could spread indefinitely through a community that regularly encounters death.
